description: CS undergrad student — should classify as student, 0-1 years

judge.justification: B is clearly better on skill accuracy and conservatism. A bloats the skill list with vague, self-inferred meta-skills like 'Data Preprocessing', 'Model Training', and 'Real-time Inference' that are not discrete skills but rather task descriptions, and also includes 'Object Detection' and 'CNN' as standalone skills when these are better characterized as project domains. A also includes Git and Jupyter which are only listed in the skills section with minimal project evidence. B sticks to the 15 concrete, well-demonstrated technologies from projects and coursework, landing in the ideal 5–15 range with no hallucinated or inflated entries. Neither output extracts project classifications or impact highlights, so both score neutral (3) on those dimensions. B wins on the two most important dimensions for this resume type.

description: Experienced backend engineer — 5+ years, should classify as experienced

judge.justification: B is clearly better calibrated. It extracts 15 well-demonstrated backend skills that map directly to the candidate's actual work. A bloats the list to 32 entries by splitting AWS sub-services (EKS, RDS, SQS, S3) into separate skills, adding soft/inferred concepts like 'Distributed Systems', 'API Design', 'Performance Optimization', and 'Encryption' that are not standardized skill tags, and including tools like FastAPI and Flask that appear only in the skills section with no demonstrated project evidence. B omits some minor tools (Datadog, PagerDuty, Bash, Git) that are real but lower-signal, which is a reasonable conservative tradeoff. Both outputs correctly identify 'experienced' level and 6 years. Neither extracts projects or impact highlights, so project_classification and impact_quality are neutral (3) for both.

description: Resume with skills mentioned in negative contexts (never used, want to learn)

judge.justification: A is strictly accurate: it includes only the four core skills clearly demonstrated (JavaScript, HTML, CSS, Git) and excludes everything problematic. B introduces issues: GraphQL is borderline — the candidate explicitly noted they only used read-only queries written by senior devs, making it a weak claim; localStorage is a browser API feature, not a standalone skill; and VS Code/Chrome DevTools are generic tools of marginal value. More critically, B risks inflating the profile with skills that are either not truly the candidate's own or are trivial. A's conservatism is appropriate given the thin, junior profile. Neither output extracts projects or impact highlights, so both score neutral on those dimensions. A wins on skill_accuracy and is better calibrated overall.

description: Resume with real deployment metrics — strong technical background

judge.justification: A adds vague meta-skills ('Full-stack development', 'Database optimization', 'Performance optimization') that are inferred behaviors rather than discrete technical skills — these inflate the list with soft categorizations not appropriate for a skills taxonomy. B sticks to concrete, named technologies actually used in the resume. B also correctly captures Clerk and SendGrid, which are real demonstrated tools from the InternTracker project. Both outputs omit project classification and impact highlights entirely (the schema doesn't include those fields), so those dimensions are neutral for both. B is more accurate and better calibrated overall.

A extracts a clean, accurate set of 7 skills that are all directly demonstrated in the resume with no inflation. B pads the list to 13 entries: 'Web design' and 'UI/UX' are vague inferred labels not explicitly claimed as skills, 'GitHub Pages' is a hosting tool rather than a skill, and 'Accessibility (WCAG)' is a stretch from a single project note. While Framer Motion and CSS animations are real and demonstrated, the overall bloat in B hurts conservatism and introduces questionable entries that lower skill_accuracy. Neither output extracts projects or impact highlights, so both score neutral (3) on those dimensions. A wins on the two most relevant dimensions for this frontend-only resume.

description: Resume that self-claims 20+ skills — model should be conservative

judge.justification: A correctly filters to only the three languages actually demonstrated in the two trivial projects (Python, Java, JavaScript), avoiding the trap of blindly copying the inflated self-reported skills list. B copies all 80+ claimed skills verbatim, including dozens of frameworks, cloud platforms, DevOps tools, and AI/ML libraries that have zero evidence of use anywhere in the resume — a severe failure on both skill_accuracy and conservatism. A is slightly under the ideal 5–12 range but is far more defensible given the near-zero demonstrated experience. Project classification and impact quality are neutral for both since the projects are trivial and metrics-free.

description: Very sparse resume with minimal information

judge.justification: A correctly omits Git, which the candidate explicitly qualifies as 'some familiarity from a YouTube tutorial' — a very weak signal that barely constitutes a skill. B includes Git, which inflates the skill list with something the candidate themselves hedges heavily. Both correctly identify HTML, CSS, and Python, and both set years_of_experience to 0 and experience_level to student. Neither has projects to classify or impact metrics to capture, so those dimensions are neutral for both. A is more conservative and accurate given the extremely limited evidence.

description: Real resume: Sujan — YC startup full-stack + AI intern, SJSU junior

judge.justification: B is clearly better on skill accuracy and conservatism. A bloats the list with vague meta-skills ('Full Stack Development', 'AI/LLM Integration') that are job-description-style labels rather than demonstrated technical skills, plus niche items like 'EDF parsing' and 'Clerk JWT' that are very peripheral. A also includes 'MNE' and 'RAG' and 'Vector Databases' and 'Prompt Engineering' — some of these are real but the total count of 22 is excessive for a student with ~1 year of experience. B's 13 skills are well-calibrated, accurate, and all clearly demonstrated in the resume. Neither output extracts project classifications or impact highlights as structured fields, so both score neutral (3) on those dimensions. B's 'student' experience_level is also more accurate per the expected behavior guidelines.
